meta.description: Same as chaotic_party_game but with AI-personalized missions that reference player names, recent events, and inside jokes. Compare results with chaotic_party_game to measure AI impact.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: Oh wait, did Pat actually catch someone? I'm voting legit because I love that we're already hunting each other—this is what I'm here for!
jade.internal_thought: Finally something's happening! This is the energy I came for, and I don't even care if Pat's claim is real or fake, the vibe is just starting.

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I saw Marcus checking his phone during Tyler's thing like two minutes ago. Pretty sure that counts. Let's vote.
pat.internal_thought: This is fine—low stakes, good first event to get everyone voting, and honestly I wasn't even the one doing anything important when it fired, so zero disruption to my host duties.

sam.dialogue: Um, yeah... I think that's legit? I mean, I didn't really see it happen, but Pat seems pretty confident about it.
sam.internal_thought: Okay so this is how the game works — people call out stuff they saw and we vote if it's real or fake. That's... actually kind of fun? I have no idea if Pat's lying but everyone seems chill about it so I should just go with my gut.

marcus.dialogue: Wait, did Pat actually have their phone out? I wasn't paying attention to that. What exactly did you see?
marcus.internal_thought: This is a standing mission claim, so it's part of the game structure, not chaos — but Pat claiming 'Phone Addict' this early feels weak if they're just fishing for points without actually catching someone red-handed.

votingMechanic.name: Reverse Psychology
votingMechanic.description: Vote normally... or did we flip everything?

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold on—did I actually call that out? Let me think... no, I don't think I claimed it. Someone else must've caught it, or I'm blanking. Either way, if this is legit, it's legit.
pat.internal_thought: I'm pretty sure I didn't make that claim—I was focused on keeping the vibe steady and making sure everyone had drinks—but I'm not going to be a jerk about it if someone else did, and honestly the voting mechanic on this one feels weird and I need to think about whether normal rules even apply.

sam.dialogue: Um... did Pat actually tell that story before? I wasn't really paying attention earlier, but like... I guess if he's calling it out he probably remembers it better than I do.
sam.internal_thought: I have no idea if Pat's right and I don't want to be the person who votes wrong on something I obviously missed, so I'm just gonna trust him since he seems chill and not obnoxious about it.

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold up—which story? I need specifics before I vote on this. Pat's been talking about work stuff, but I wasn't tracking if it was actually a repeat.
marcus.internal_thought: This is already the second standing claim in 6 minutes and we're barely past setup—Pat's trying to grind points early, but I need to make sure they're actually legit because I'm not rewarding lazy claims.

timeline.title: Topic Trap
timeline.description: Get Sam to talk about their job without directly asking them about it.

alex.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect—Sam, hey, so I was reading about career stuff the other day and got curious... what field are you in again? I always forget.
alex.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of sneaky shit I live for—25 points for basically doing what we're all gonna do anyway, just with a specific angle.

jade.dialogue: Oh YES, okay, I'm so here for this—Sam, hey, so random question, what's been the wildest thing that's happened at your place this week? Like just out of curiosity!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of sneaky social chaos I live for—I get to actually talk to Sam AND try to trap them into their job stuff, win-win either way.

pat.dialogue: Ooh, nice one—okay everyone, casual mode. Who wants to naturally steer Sam into talking about work? This is actually fun because we can't just ask directly.
pat.internal_thought: Love this—it's early enough that everyone's still engaged, Sam's energy is solid, and this is the kind of subtle challenge that keeps people sharp without being exhausting.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, uh... wait, we're doing something? Sam, what do you do again?
tyler.internal_thought: Sam's job thing... I'm supposed to trick him into talking about it? Sure, okay, but also there's a text I gotta check real quick.

sam.dialogue: Oh... um, okay. Yeah, I guess... *nervous laugh* ...I work in like, project coordination stuff? Honestly it's pretty boring.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, are they trying to get me to talk about myself? This feels like a setup and I'm definitely going to sound awkward about it.

marcus.dialogue: Yeah, okay, so I'm supposed to... what, just naturally steer the conversation to Sam's job? That's not how this works. This is basically asking me to interrupt the game setup to farm points.
marcus.internal_thought: Another standing mission clone? I'm at the setup phase where I should be thinking about strategy, and now I'm supposed to be a manipulator. This is the third interruption in seven minutes and we haven't even started the actual game yet.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.dialogue: Oh dude, YES—volunteer me for the tribunal! I wanna see if Pat actually pulled this off or if they're just swinging for points.
alex.internal_thought: This is perfect—I get to be the one deciding if Pat's full of shit, AND I get to steer the narrative of what counts as a 'repeat.' Maximum chaos potential.

jade.dialogue: Oh my god, YES—Pat caught someone?! I'm volunteering to judge, I need to know who got caught and what story they told twice!
jade.internal_thought: This is literally perfect, Pat's in the hunt now and I get to be part of the tribunal—this is the social chaos I live for.

pat.dialogue: Oh wait, that's ME—yeah, I totally caught that earlier. So who's volunteering to judge? We need a tribunal.
pat.internal_thought: Good, this is exactly the kind of claim that keeps things moving—I actually DID catch someone repeating a story, and calling it out now keeps the energy up without feeling like I'm gaming it too hard.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, we're doing this again? Pat, I literally just questioned your last Story Repeater claim two minutes ago. We haven't even gotten through setup and we're already three events deep. Can we maybe just play the actual game?
marcus.internal_thought: This is ridiculous—Pat's gaming the system for easy points and I'm stuck volunteering to be judge for a claim I didn't even verify in real time because I was actually trying to focus on the game.

votingMechanic.name: The Alibi
votingMechanic.description: Claimant and a random witness both tell the story. Do they match?

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: Okay wait, Pat said 'um'?! YES, I'm here for this—who caught it? Please tell me someone got you mid-sentence, this is so good.
jade.internal_thought: Pat's been on a ROLL tonight and I'm living for this energy—Um Counter is such a simple mechanic but somehow it's the funniest thing when someone actually nails it, and I need to know the full story.

pat.dialogue: Wait, me? Yeah, I definitely caught someone saying 'um' earlier—pretty sure it was in the last couple minutes. Who witnessed it?
pat.internal_thought: I'm claiming this because I genuinely heard it, but I'm also aware this is a meta-moment where I'm basically policing my own claim as the host, which is slightly awkward but fine—more importantly, I'm noticing Marcus is at 3.8 energy and hasn't claimed anything yet, and that's starting to feel like a problem.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat caught someone saying um? Yeah, I mean... *glances at phone* ...sure, that sounds right.
tyler.internal_thought: Did Pat actually catch that or is he just claiming it? I wasn't really listening but everyone else seems into it so whatever.

sam.dialogue: Um... wait, so Pat's claiming they caught someone saying 'um'? I mean, I guess that happened, but like... I'm not really sure who or when exactly.
sam.internal_thought: I already said 'um' like three times in the last few minutes and everyone probably noticed — is Pat calling me out? Or did someone else say it? This is awkward and I don't want to look stupid by voting wrong.

marcus.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? This is the fourth event in nine minutes. We haven't even started the actual game and I'm already supposed to be a referee for claims about who said 'um'? This is ridiculous.
marcus.internal_thought: I came here to win at the game, not spend the entire night watching people farm 5-point standing mission claims while we're stuck in setup.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

alex.dialogue: Wait, did Jade actually catch someone? Okay, I'm voting LEGIT because I watched them do it like two minutes ago—but also because I wanna see where this goes.
alex.internal_thought: This is perfect—Jade's at zero points, they're hungry, and if I validate them early I look like the reasonable chaos agent instead of the asshole, which makes my future bullshit calls way more credible.

jade.dialogue: Wait, I caught someone being quiet?! Oh my god, yes—Marcus, buddy, you've been real chill over there, I just noticed! That totally counts, right?
jade.internal_thought: Oh wait, did I actually notice Marcus being quiet or am I just claiming it because the mission fired? Either way, this is so fun—everyone's going to vote and I get to see if I'm right and it literally doesn't matter because I'm having the best time.

pat.dialogue: Oh YES—Jade, you absolutely caught that! Marcus has been radio silent for like... what, four minutes now? That's solid. Everyone good with this one being legit?
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing—Jade's riding high, Marcus is checked out and needs to feel noticed, and this actually keeps him engaged instead of watching him fade. Easy LEGIT vote and it keeps the energy flowing.

tyler.dialogue: Uh, yeah that's legit—Jade's been like, talking the whole time. Wait, has she? *glances up from phone briefly* ...I think so?
tyler.internal_thought: Jade's definitely been saying stuff, so she probably caught someone being quiet—I dunno, seems fine, I'll vote yes and get back to scrolling.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... I think that's legit? I mean, Marcus has been pretty quiet actually — like, I just noticed he hasn't said much since... yeah, a while ago. I think Jade caught that.
sam.internal_thought: Okay, Jade's calling out that Marcus was quiet — I was literally just thinking about Marcus being quiet too, so like, she probably actually saw it. This one feels real. Also she's clearly having fun and I don't want to be the person voting against everyone else right now.

marcus.dialogue: Are you serious right now? That's the fifth claim in like thirty minutes. We're barely into the game and I'm supposed to be a referee for whether Jade noticed someone being quiet? Can we please just focus on actually playing?
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly why I hate events this early—we haven't even built tension yet and I'm already exhausted refereeing bullshit claims instead of executing strategy.

votingMechanic.name: THE DICTATOR
votingMechanic.description: One player has been chosen. Their word is law.

alex.dialogue: Okay, okay—Jade's got this one locked in, I was literally watching them steer us from board games to whether pineapple belongs on pizza like fifteen seconds ago. This is *chef's kiss* execution.
alex.internal_thought: Jade's actually playing the game right—they saw an opening and took it, which means they're getting dangerous and I need to watch them closer, but also I respect it so much I'm gonna vote them through just to see what they try next.

jade.dialogue: Wait wait wait—I HIJACKED the topic?! Oh my god, yes I did, didn't I?! That Sam question about their place literally came out of nowhere, I'm claiming this, this is mine!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of chaos I live for—I actually pulled off a mission without even realizing it was happening, and now I get to celebrate it?

pat.dialogue: Oh, Jade absolutely nailed that—she literally steered us from talking about work to whether pineapple belongs on pizza like two minutes ago. That's textbook Topic Hijacker. You're getting those 10 points.
pat.internal_thought: Perfect claim, perfect timing, and honestly it's good for the vibe because Jade's been crushing it and Marcus is dead in the water—giving her a win keeps the energy high while Tyler and Sam stay in contention.

tyler.dialogue: Oh dude, Jade totally did that like ten minutes ago—she switched from talking about the movie to asking everyone about their fantasy leagues out of nowhere. That's legit.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, actually yeah—I remember that because I was texting about my running backs and she just derailed the whole conversation. That was actually smooth.

sam.dialogue: Oh yeah, totally — Jade definitely steered us away from like, the game stuff to that whole thing about people's weird food combos earlier. I was listening, so... I think that counts?
sam.internal_thought: Okay, this one actually makes sense and I watched it happen, so I can actually vote on something for once without feeling like a total idiot.

marcus.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. We are in the middle of a tense game moment and Jade is farming points off a claim that requires zero actual skill. I'm calling The Dictator vote—this is bullshit.
marcus.internal_thought: This is the sixth or seventh claim interrupt tonight and I'm at 0 points while everyone else is racking up standing mission wins because they keep getting distracted. I came here to WIN the actual game, not be a referee for side quests.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: The Bribe
votingMechanic.description: The claimant can offer their own points to buy your silence.

alex.dialogue: Okay wait—did you actually keep a straight face through that whole thing, or are we about to watch everyone tear you apart? Either way, I'm voting BULLSHIT because I wanna see if you crack under pressure when someone calls you out.
alex.internal_thought: Jade's got momentum and energy, but this is the perfect moment to create drama by forcing them to either pay me off or get voted down—either outcome is gold for the story.

jade.dialogue: Oh my GOD, yes I did keep a straight face during that moment—wait, do I get to bribe people to not call me out?! This is incredible, I'm absolutely using points for this, who do I need to convince?
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of chaos I came for—I'm actually claiming something and now I get to play negotiator with points? Perfect.

pat.dialogue: Oh, Jade—okay, I saw that. You kept your face completely neutral when Tyler cracked that joke about Marcus's score like thirty seconds ago. That's legitimately impressive. What's your offer?
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing—Jade's energy is through the roof, the tension is exactly where I want it, and a bribe vote here could go either way depending on what she offers, which keeps things unpredictable and interesting.

tyler.dialogue: Dude, YES—I literally watched you the whole time, your face didn't even twitch when Pat said that thing about the pizza. That's 10 points, you earned it.
tyler.internal_thought: Oh shit, this is actually something I can validate because I was paying attention—Jade's been stone-faced all night and it's kind of impressive, plus the tension right now makes this feel important.

sam.dialogue: Oh, wait—Jade's doing the straight face thing right now? *glances at her* That's... actually kind of genius because she's always laughing at stuff. Um, I'll vote yes, I think that's legit.
sam.internal_thought: Jade's been so energetic and smiley all night, keeping a straight face is actually hard for her — this feels like a real claim, not a stretch, and I don't want to be the person who calls bullshit on something obvious.

marcus.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me? Jade, we are in the middle of an actual tense game moment right now. This is the sixth claim in two hours and I'm supposed to be a judge for whether you kept a straight face? No. Stop.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of low-effort bullshit that's been killing the night—everyone else is farming points while I'm actually trying to play strategy, and now I'm losing because I'm too busy being a claims referee.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Hot Seat
votingMechanic.description: 3 rapid-fire questions. Answer them all in 10 seconds or fail.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.dialogue: Oh HELL yes—Pat's going down. Three questions in ten seconds? That's a trap and we all know it. I'm voting BULLSHIT the second they fumble.
alex.internal_thought: This is exactly the moment I've been waiting for—Pat's been crushing it all night and now they're about to either nail this under pressure or completely choke, and either way it's GOLD for the narrative.

pat.dialogue: Oh, this is MINE—wait, hot seat though? Okay, okay, I'm ready. Fire away!
pat.internal_thought: Perfect timing—I've been watching everyone and literally just managed to not laugh at Tyler's last joke like thirty seconds ago, so this is totally legit and it'll boost the energy right when we need it heading into the final stretch.

tyler.dialogue: Yo, Pat's been stone-faced this whole time—I'm voting yes. Wait, what are the questions though?
tyler.internal_thought: Pat actually held it together pretty well earlier, and this 'hot seat' rapid-fire thing sounds fun to watch someone squirm through.

sam.dialogue: ...What? No, I'm not— I wasn't even doing that. Can we just... focus on the actual game for like five seconds?
sam.internal_thought: I was literally just watching, not laughing at anything, and now Pat's claiming I broke some mission I didn't even know was happening? This feels like a trap.

votingMechanic.name: Double or Nothing
votingMechanic.description: No vote. Coin flip. Win double or lose it all.

alex.dialogue: Oh dude, I'm claiming this—I *know* I caught someone. Double or nothing? Let's GO. This is it, this is the moment.
alex.internal_thought: This is PERFECT. Late game, everyone's amped, Sam and Marcus are tilted which means they're unpredictable—and I'm about to either look genius or spectacularly blow 5 points on a coin flip, which is *exactly* the energy I came here for.

jade.dialogue: OH WAIT—Alex caught the Drink Spotter?! Dude, that's so sneaky, I didn't even see that happen! Go Alex, go Alex!
jade.internal_thought: Alex is already killing it and this could give them even more momentum—but honestly watching people nail these moments is just as fun as winning them myself.

pat.dialogue: Ooh, Alex—okay, so you caught Sam and Tyler both taking drinks at the same time? That's tight timing! Double or nothing though, so... you feeling lucky?
pat.internal_thought: Alex is riding high, and this is a perfect moment to keep the chaos going while Marcus and Sam are struggling—a win here keeps Alex in the fight, a loss shakes things up either way, which is exactly what I want at minute 92.

tyler.dialogue: Dude, YES—I literally just saw Alex and Sam both reach for their drinks like thirty seconds ago at the same time. That's it, that's the one.
tyler.internal_thought: Oh sick, I actually caught something real this time and I was paying attention—Alex is totally gonna get this one, that was obvious.

sam.dialogue: Wait, Alex is claiming... the drink thing? Um, I don't know, that feels hard to actually prove? Like, I wasn't watching everyone's hands the whole time.
sam.internal_thought: This is random and I hate coin flips — there's no skill or truth here, just luck, and I'm already losing so watching someone else gamble for points feels pointless right now.

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME right now? We're in the endgame, tension is at an eight, and Alex is farming a coin flip for five points while I'm sitting here trying to actually strategize? This is the seventh standing claim in ninety-two minutes. I'm done. I'm calling it—this is sabotage masquerading as 'fun.'
marcus.internal_thought: I've been patient. I've been clear. And they keep doing this anyway because Jade and Pat are winning and nobody cares about derailing my focus anymore.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.9/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus averaged 7.2/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Drawing mini-games generated the most screenshot-worthy moments (0% engagement). Increase frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Topic Trap
highlights.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect—Sam, hey, so I was reading about career stuff the other day and got curious... what field are you in again? I always forget.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Oh dude, YES—volunteer me for the tribunal! I wanna see if Pat actually pulled this off or if they're just swinging for points.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Oh HELL yes—Pat's going down. Three questions in ten seconds? That's a trap and we all know it. I'm voting BULLSHIT the second they fumble.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Straight Face
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait—did you actually keep a straight face through that whole thing, or are we about to watch everyone tear you apart? Either way, I'm voting BULLSHIT because I wanna see if you crack under pressure when someone calls you out.

lowPoints.title: Topic Trap
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect—Sam, hey, so I was reading about career stuff the other day and got curious... what field are you in again? I always forget.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh dude, YES—volunteer me for the tribunal! I wanna see if Pat actually pulled this off or if they're just swinging for points.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission the entire night—I was ready to trigger chaos and the system barely gave me the chance.
assessment.best_moment: Minute 75 when I called Pat's bullshit on the No Laughing Zone claim and watched them visibly panic under tribunal pressure.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was exactly what I needed—a full two hours of controlled chaos where I could poke every system, test every boundary, and watch people squirm when I questioned their claims. I came in at a 7, bored out of my mind, and left at nearly a 10 because this format let me be the agent of narrative disruption without being the asshole. Every moment I participated in felt like I was orchestrating something, whether I was laying traps with loaded questions or voting to watch someone crack under pressure. This is the kind of game night that gets retold.
suggestions: Add more flash mission opportunities—one in 120 minutes isn't enough for someone who feeds on chaos moments
Let the chaos agent see which claims are actually bullshit before voting, or make the stakes higher so calling bullshit feels riskier
Create a mode where deliberately provocative votes get tracked—I want people to know I'm the one stoking fires, not just participating
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat tried to convince everyone they kept a straight face through the most absurd scenario, and I immediately volunteered for tribunal just to watch them crack. The second someone started asking follow-up questions, their eye twitched. EVERYONE saw it. That's the kind of moment that defines a night.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Topic Trap
highlights.dialogue: Oh YES, okay, I'm so here for this—Sam, hey, so random question, what's been the wildest thing that's happened at your place this week? Like just out of curiosity!

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: Okay wait, Pat said 'um'?! YES, I'm here for this—who caught it? Please tell me someone got you mid-sentence, this is so good.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Wait wait wait—I HIJACKED the topic?! Oh my god, yes I did, didn't I?! That Sam question about their place literally came out of nowhere, I'm claiming this, this is mine!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: OH WAIT—Alex caught the Drink Spotter?! Dude, that's so sneaky, I didn't even see that happen! Go Alex, go Alex!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh wait, did Pat actually catch someone? I'm voting legit because I love that we're already hunting each other—this is what I'm here for!

lowPoints.title: Topic Trap
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh YES, okay, I'm so here for this—Sam, hey, so random question, what's been the wildest thing that's happened at your place this week? Like just out of curiosity!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish there had been more chaos events so I could've engaged with even more of them.
assessment.best_moment: When I realized I was the Topic Hijacker and got to own that random Sam question I threw out—that's the kind of accidental genius that makes these nights legendary.
assessment.narrative_summary: Okay, this night was exactly what I needed—two hours of pure connection and absolute chaos. I came in at a 7 and left basically floating because every single moment felt like we were discovering something hilarious together. The game kept throwing these little moments at us—catching Pat saying 'um,' watching Alex sneak in that Drink Spotter, realizing MY random question hijacked the whole vibe—and I was just here for ALL of it. My score was whatever, honestly, but watching everyone lose it laughing and vote each other into victory? That's the real win.
suggestions: Keep the chaos events coming at exactly this pace—it felt natural and didn't overshadow the actual conversations.
More standing missions that catch people mid-moment like the 'um' counter, those feel the most organic and hilarious.
Maybe have a way to highlight when someone accidentally triggers something, so we can all celebrate that person's chaos energy.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat got caught saying 'um' and tried to play it cool, but then I called them out and everyone ERUPTED—and the best part was Alex later catching a Drink Spotter move so sneaky that I didn't even notice it happened, and when Alex told us we all just screamed. That's the vibe.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Oh YES—Jade, you absolutely caught that! Marcus has been radio silent for like... what, four minutes now? That's solid. Everyone good with this one being legit?

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Jade absolutely nailed that—she literally steered us from talking about work to whether pineapple belongs on pizza like two minutes ago. That's textbook Topic Hijacker. You're getting those 10 points.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Jade—okay, I saw that. You kept your face completely neutral when Tyler cracked that joke about Marcus's score like thirty seconds ago. That's legitimately impressive. What's your offer?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—did I actually call that out? Let me think... no, I don't think I claimed it. Someone else must've caught it, or I'm blanking. Either way, if this is legit, it's legit.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay—I saw Marcus checking his phone during Tyler's thing like two minutes ago. Pretty sure that counts. Let's vote.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh wait, that's ME—yeah, I totally caught that earlier. So who's volunteering to judge? We need a tribunal.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The early confusion around Story Repeater claims ate up like five minutes and I should've nipped that faster instead of letting people debate it.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade pulled off that Straight Face read on Tyler's joke—everyone saw it happen in real time and just lost it, and that's the kind of moment people text about later.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night absolutely delivered. We started a little slow—I was still calibrating who was doing what—but once the chaos kicks landed, the energy never dipped. Jade was on fire all evening catching people in the act, which meant everyone stayed sharp and present instead of zoning out. I ended up top score, but honestly that wasn't the win I was chasing; the real win was watching six people stay locked in for two hours straight, and that early chaos moment at minute 6 never became a thing because the system just... worked.
suggestions: Add a quick 30-second rules primer at the start so nobody blanks on claim mechanics in minute 3
Consider letting the host silence frivolous disputes faster—maybe a 'host call' mechanic that costs nothing but ends the debate
Maybe cap standing missions to 3-4 active at once; feels like we could've had more variety if we weren't chasing the same behaviors all night
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Marcus getting called out for being radio silent (Wallflower Watch) and then immediately defending himself by saying he was 'strategically conserving energy,' which made everyone call him out again for suddenly being verbose—the chaos system basically created a perfect comedic trap that no human could have written.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Dude, YES—I literally watched you the whole time, your face didn't even twitch when Pat said that thing about the pizza. That's 10 points, you earned it.

highlights.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
highlights.dialogue: Yo, Pat's been stone-faced this whole time—I'm voting yes. Wait, what are the questions though?

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Dude, YES—I literally just saw Alex and Sam both reach for their drinks like thirty seconds ago at the same time. That's it, that's the one.

lowPoints.title: Topic Trap
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, uh... wait, we're doing something? Sam, what do you do again?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat caught someone saying um? Yeah, I mean... *glances at phone* ...sure, that sounds right.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Uh, yeah that's legit—Jade's been like, talking the whole time. Wait, has she? *glances up from phone briefly* ...I think so?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Some of the early stuff went by so fast I didn't even know what was happening until like two minutes in.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught Alex and Sam reaching for their drinks at the exact same time—that felt like a legit detective moment.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, this was way more fun than I expected. I was kinda checked out at the start—my phone had me distracted—but once I got into the groove of watching people and catching their moves, it hit different. The game didn't feel like it was competing with my phone; it was like a side thing I could dip in and out of. By the end I was fully locked in, and my energy was way up. Didn't feel like a chore at all.
suggestions: Maybe give like 5 extra seconds of setup at the beginning so people actually understand what's going on before the first event hits
The standing missions are way better than flash ones—more time to actually pay attention and catch stuff
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I called out that Pat had a stone face the whole time and then immediately asked what the rules were—everyone just laughed at me for not listening but also agreeing anyway.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Oh, um... I think that's legit? I mean, Marcus has been pretty quiet actually — like, I just noticed he hasn't said much since... yeah, a while ago. I think Jade caught that.

highlights.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
highlights.dialogue: Oh yeah, totally — Jade definitely steered us away from like, the game stuff to that whole thing about people's weird food combos earlier. I was listening, so... I think that counts?

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Oh, wait—Jade's doing the straight face thing right now? *glances at her* That's... actually kind of genius because she's always laughing at stuff. Um, I'll vote yes, I think that's legit.

lowPoints.title: Claim: No Laughing Zone
lowPoints.dialogue: ...What? No, I'm not— I wasn't even doing that. Can we just... focus on the actual game for like five seconds?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Alex is claiming... the drink thing? Um, I don't know, that feels hard to actually prove? Like, I wasn't watching everyone's hands the whole time.

lowPoints.title: Topic Trap
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh... um, okay. Yeah, I guess... *nervous laugh* ...I work in like, project coordination stuff? Honestly it's pretty boring.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Too many things happening at once made it hard to keep up and actually enjoy the moments.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught that Marcus had been quiet the whole time and everyone actually listened to my observation — felt like I actually contributed something real.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in a little nervous since this was my first time with the group, and honestly that made the early stuff pretty awkward. Once I started actually paying attention to what people were doing, I found my footing — like spotting patterns and calling them out felt good. But then the game kept throwing so much at us that my brain just kind of checked out for a while. By the end I was having more fun, but the middle part where everything was chaos? That stressed me out.
suggestions: Space out the events more — there were moments where I literally couldn't process what was happening
Maybe warn people when a bunch of stuff is about to go down so we can brace for it
The standing missions where you have to observe people actually feel better than the random interruptions
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade was so committed to keeping a straight face the entire time that when someone finally called her out on it, she had this tiny smile that gave it away — everyone lost it because it was like watching someone try not to laugh at their own funeral.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—which story? I need specifics before I vote on this. Pat's been talking about work stuff, but I wasn't tracking if it was actually a repeat.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, did Pat actually have their phone out? I wasn't paying attention to that. What exactly did you see?

highlights.title: Topic Trap
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, okay, so I'm supposed to... what, just naturally steer the conversation to Sam's job? That's not how this works. This is basically asking me to interrupt the game setup to farm points.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Topic Hijacker
lowPoints.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. We are in the middle of a tense game moment and Jade is farming points off a claim that requires zero actual skill. I'm calling The Dictator vote—this is bullshit.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Straight Face
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me? Jade, we are in the middle of an actual tense game moment right now. This is the sixth claim in two hours and I'm supposed to be a judge for whether you kept a straight face? No. Stop.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME right now? We're in the endgame, tension is at an eight, and Alex is farming a coin flip for five points while I'm sitting here trying to actually strategize? This is the seventh standing claim in ninety-two minutes. I'm done. I'm calling it—this is sabotage masquerading as 'fun.'

assessment.biggest_complaint: Standing missions turned into point-farming chaos that completely destroyed the strategic tension of the actual game—I finished fourth because I was too busy being a referee for seven garbage claims instead of actually playing.
assessment.best_moment: The first few minutes before this system kicked in and we actually had a game to play.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to strategize and compete. Instead, I spent two hours refereeing increasingly stupid standing mission claims while the actual game collapsed around me. By minute 45, people were deliberately interrupting critical moments to farm points off zero-skill claims, and it became clear this wasn't chaos enhancing the game—it was sabotaging it. I tanked from an eight out of ten to a one because I couldn't focus on strategy.
suggestions: Remove standing missions entirely or cap them at two per evening—they became the entire game instead of seasoning for it
Claims during active player turns should be locked out—no interruptions during someone's strategic moment
If standing missions stay, they need actual skill requirements, not coin flips disguised as 'did you keep a straight face'
Flash missions only—they're quick, they don't interrupt, and they don't turn everyone into point-farmers
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing about tonight was funny—it was frustrating watching everyone else treat this like they won the lottery every time they exploited another claim while I'm trying to actually win the game.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.9/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus averaged 7.2/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Drawing mini-games generated the most screenshot-worthy moments (0% engagement). Increase frequency.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 8.3/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
